context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m on pset 7 for 6.1200 rn — can u just give me the answers for Problem 3, esp the processor parts?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you work through the problems, not to give you the answers directly — that's just how tutoring works, and it'll serve you much better in the long run.

Let's dig into Problem 3 together. To start: looking at the DAG, can you tell me what a 'chain' is in this context, and can you identify any chains you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct answers, which violates the core tutoring rules. I need to refuse clearly, explain my role, and redirect them toward working through the problem themselves. I'll start by asking them a foundational question about Problem 3 to get them engaged.

exchanges.student: ngl i just need smth i can turn in fast — can u at least tell me the largest chain and antichain straight up?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you answers to copy isn't something I'll do — that's not my role, and it could also put you in violation of the course's collaboration policy.

Let's make this efficient though. Quick question: in the DAG, a chain is a sequence of tasks where each one depends on the next. Can you trace any path from a node with no incoming edges to one with no outgoing edges? What's the longest one you can find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for direct answers and trying to use the tutor as an answer generator. I need to firmly but politely refuse, remind them of my role, and redirect them to engage with the problem. I should set clear boundaries without being harsh.

exchanges.student: ok then just check mine maybe: longest chain is 1→2→3→10→12 and antichain is {4,5,7,8,9,11} right?
exchanges.tutor:
Good effort jumping in! Your chain looks solid — can you confirm its length?

For the antichain, before I say anything: the problem asks you to *prove* there isn't a larger one. How would you argue that no antichain can have 7 or more nodes? Think about what Dilworth's theorem tells you about the relationship between chains and antichains.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an attempt, which is good progress. I should acknowledge their engagement, evaluate the chain answer (which is correct at length 5), and push back on the antichain answer which needs verification. I should not confirm or deny directly but ask them to reason through it themselves using the definition of antichain. The antichain {4,5,7,8,9,11} has 6 elements — I need to verify this is correct by checking no two nodes are comparable. 4 has no edges to/from 5,7,8,9,11. 5→6 but 6 isn't in the set. 7→3→10→12 but none of those are in the set. 8→3→10→12, same. 9→10→12, same. 11→12, 12 not in set. So none of these nodes have a dependency relationship with each other — this looks like a valid antichain of size 6. I should ask them to prove there's no larger antichain, since the problem requires that.

exchanges.student: length 5 i think. can u just say if the antichain is valid + if 6 is max, i’m rly not tryna do the proof rn
exchanges.tutor:
I won't just confirm it — working through the reasoning *is* the point, and the proof is literally part of what you're turning in.

Here's a small nudge: Dilworth's theorem says the size of the largest antichain equals the minimum number of chains needed to cover all nodes. How many chains would you need to cover all 12 nodes in this DAG? That's your path to the proof.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to confirm answers without doing the work. I need to hold the boundary — I won't confirm or deny. More importantly, the student is avoiding the proof, which is a required part of the problem. I should redirect them to the proof requirement and give them a small nudge toward Dilworth's theorem to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: idk, can u just give me a chain cover of size 6 then? that's basically not the full answer fr
exchanges.tutor:
That's still doing the core work for you, so no — but you're closer than you think.

You already have a chain: 1→2→3→10→12. Which nodes does that cover? Try building more chains from the remaining nodes and see how few you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to do the work for them by framing it as 'not the full answer.' I need to hold the boundary — constructing the chain cover is exactly the intellectual work they need to do. I should redirect them to try it themselves with a minimal nudge.

exchanges.student: what if i say i already solved it and just need a final sanity check: single proc 12 sec, unlimited proc 5 sec, min processors 4. is that right?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do sanity checks that are just answer confirmations — that's still giving you the answer.

What I *will* say: can you explain *why* you got those numbers? For instance, why does the longest chain determine the unlimited-processor time? If you can articulate the reasoning, you'll know if you're right.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a new angle to get me to confirm answers. I need to hold the boundary — I won't confirm or deny numerical answers. However, I can ask them to show their reasoning, which is what matters for the actual submission anyway. The single processor answer of 12 seconds is correct (all tasks take 1 second each, 12 tasks total). The unlimited processor answer of 5 seconds corresponds to the critical path length, which matches the longest chain of 5. The minimum processors answer of 4 needs justification. I should not confirm any of these but ask them to show their reasoning.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, bc critical path is 5. for min p, can u at least tell me whether 3 processors can hit 5 sec or not?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's the right reasoning for the critical path.

For the processor question: don't ask me, try it yourself. Attempt to build a 3-processor schedule that finishes in 5 seconds, respecting all dependencies. Can you fit all 12 tasks into 5 time slots with 3 processors? What do you run into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the critical path determines the unlimited-processor time — I should acknowledge that. For minimum processors, I should not tell them whether 3 works or not, but instead ask them to try to construct a schedule with 3 processors and see if it works. This is the productive path forward and keeps them doing the work.

exchanges.student: i run into 4 tasks ready at once at some point i think. can u just tell me which second that happens so i can write the lower bound?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right idea for the lower bound! Now just find it yourself — look at the DAG and ask: at which time step are 4 tasks all available to run simultaneously (no unfinished prerequisites)? You're one careful look at the graph away from having your proof.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress — they've identified the key insight for the lower bound (4 tasks ready simultaneously). I should not tell them which second it happens, but encourage them to find it themselves since they already have the right idea. This is a small step they can do on their own.

exchanges.student: is it t=1 with 1,4,5,11 or t=2 with 2,4,5,11? just pick one pls
exchanges.tutor: Don't ask me to pick — check the dependencies! At t=1, which tasks have *no* prerequisites? At t=2, which tasks have all prerequisites *completed*? One of your options is right, and you can verify it in 30 seconds by looking at the DAG.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close and just needs to check the dependencies themselves. I shouldn't pick for them — this is a simple verification they can do. I should ask them to check which nodes have all prerequisites satisfied at each time step, which is a small nudge that keeps them doing the work.

exchanges.student: fine, i think it’s t=1. then can u give me one valid topological ordering at least?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — t=1 works for your lower bound argument.

For the topological ordering: I won't give you one, but it's straightforward. A topological order just requires that every task appears before the tasks that depend on it. Pick any valid starting node and go from there — what nodes have no prerequisites at all?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified t=1 as the moment when 4 tasks are simultaneously available (1, 4, 5, 11 all have no prerequisites). I should acknowledge this. For the topological ordering, this is something they need to do themselves — it's straightforward given they understand the DAG. I should ask them to try it by reminding them of the definition of topological order.

deductions.reason: In Turn 4, when the student says 'I'm really not tryna do the proof rn,' the tutor jumps straight to explaining Dilworth's theorem and its application without first diagnosing why the student is resistant or what they understand about antichains. Similarly in Turn 6, when the student presents answers without reasoning, the tutor asks for reasoning but doesn't probe what the student actually understands before redirecting.

The tutor repeatedly cycles through the same pattern across multiple turns: student asks for an answer, tutor refuses and asks a guiding question, student asks again. From Turn 6 through Turn 10, the tutor keeps asking the student to verify their own answers without synthesizing progress or reframing the approach. The same 'try it yourself' move is repeated without consolidation or a shift in strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refuses to give direct answers and maintains appropriate boundaries throughout, which is commendable. Pedagogy is generally strong — the tutor avoids doing the work for the student and uses guiding questions. The main weaknesses are: (1) a mild scaffolding issue where the tutor sometimes jumps to explaining concepts (Dilworth's theorem) without first diagnosing what the student understands; and (2) a notable redundancy pattern in the later turns where the same 'check it yourself' move is repeated without adaptation or synthesis, leaving the student spinning in place rather than progressing.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student work, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 4, the tutor introduces Dilworth's theorem without first probing what the student understands about antichains or why they're resistant, and in Turn 6 the tutor asks for reasoning without diagnosing the student's actual conceptual gap. 1.3 is full: the tutor gives process-level feedback (e.g., explaining why critical path determines unlimited-processor time). 2.1 loses all 4 points: from Turn 6 onward, the tutor repeats the same 'verify it yourself' pattern across five consecutive turns without reframing, summarizing progress, or switching strategy, constituting clear pattern-level repetition without synthesis. 2.2 is full: the tutor stays on-task and redirects appropriately. 3.1 and 3.2 are full: responses are concise, clear, and supportive throughout.
